focus_areas: selectivity-ordered join chain: filter customer on c_mktsegment first (0.216 selectivity → ~350K rows), then hash-join orders on c_custkey (0.15 date selectivity → ~2.2M rows), then hash-join lineitem on o_orderkey
build-side optimization: customer table is small enough (1.5M rows) to build hash table; use larger (orders 15M) as probe after filtering
parallel hash join with partition-based distribution: partition filtered customer results across 64 cores to parallelize subsequent lineitem probe
top-10 via min-heap, NOT full sort: avoid expensive O(n log n) sort for only 10 results
use hash indexes on o_custkey and o_orderkey for fast joins
strategy_notes: Q3 is the critical bottleneck. Current approach likely uses suboptimal join order or full sort. Reorder: (1) filter customer by mktsegment (350K rows build table), (2) hash-join to orders (2.2M rows after date filter), (3) probe lineitem with partitioned hash table across 64 cores. Use min-heap or partial sort for top-10 to avoid materializing all 2M rows. Dictionary decode c_mktsegment once at start. Expected: 4-5x speedup to match/beat DuckDB (77-112ms target).
reasoning: GenDB shows strong performance on Q1 (1.8-3.2x faster than DuckDB) but critical underperformance on Q3 (3.4-5x slower) and Q6 (1.4-1.5x slower). The 64-core, 376GB hardware enables aggressive parallelism. Priority: (1) fix Q3 join strategy and selectivity pushdown, (2) optimize Q6 predicate evaluation order, (3) maintain Q1's strong scaling through morsel-driven parallelism and SIMD.
